name: Root
description: The root of the entire filesystem hierarchy. Everything lives under this directory.

name: Binaries
description: Essential command binaries needed in single-user mode (ls, cp, mv, bash, cat, etc.). Often symlinked to /usr/bin on modern distros.

name: Boot
description: Static files for the boot loader (GRUB/systemd-boot), kernel images (vmlinuz), and initramfs.

name: Device files
description: Virtual filesystem containing device nodes (block and character devices). Populated by udev at runtime.

name: Configuration
description: Host-specific system-wide configuration files and scripts. Contains passwd, shadow, fstab, hostname, hosts, and more.

name: Home directories
description: Per-user home directories, each owned by the respective user. Contains personal files, dotfiles, and user config.

name: Libraries
description: Essential shared libraries and kernel modules needed by /bin and /sbin. Often symlinked to /usr/lib on modern distros.

name: 64-bit Libraries
description: 64-bit shared libraries on multilib systems. Often symlinked to /usr/lib64.

name: Removable media
description: Mount point for removable media such as USB drives, CD-ROMs, and external disks. Automounted by udisks2.

name: Mount point
description: Temporary mount point for manually mounted filesystems (NFS shares, disk images, etc.).

name: Optional packages
description: Self-contained third-party software packages that do not conform to the standard FHS layout (e.g., Google Chrome, Steam).

name: Process info
description: Virtual filesystem exposing kernel and process data as files. /proc/cpuinfo, /proc/meminfo, /proc/<pid>/* etc.

name: Root home
description: Home directory of the root (superuser) account. Separate from /home for security in single-user mode.

name: Runtime data
description: Runtime variable data (PIDs, sockets, lock files) written since last boot. Cleared at boot. Replaces /var/run.

name: System binaries
description: Essential system administration binaries (fsck, ip, mount, iptables). Often symlinked to /usr/sbin on modern distros.

name: Service data
description: Data served by system services: web server content, FTP data, etc. Rarely used outside server deployments.

name: Sysfs
description: Virtual filesystem exposing the kernel's device tree and hardware information. Used by udev and hardware tools.

name: Temporary files
description: Temporary files created by programs. Typically cleared on reboot (often a tmpfs mount). World-writable with sticky bit.

name: User programs
description: Secondary hierarchy for read-only user data — most program binaries, libraries, headers, and documentation live here.

name: User binaries
description: Non-essential command binaries for all users (python, git, vim, curl, etc.).

name: Header files
description: Standard C/C++ header files for system libraries (stdio.h, stdlib.h, etc.).

name: User libraries
description: Libraries for /usr/bin and /usr/sbin. Also contains architecture-independent data files for installed packages.

name: Local hierarchy
description: Tertiary hierarchy for software compiled and installed locally by the admin (not managed by the package manager).

name: Shared data
description: Architecture-independent data shared by all packages: man pages, icons, locale data, doc files.

name: Variable data
description: Variable data that changes during normal operation: logs, spools, mail, PID files, databases.

name: Cache
description: Application cache data that can be safely deleted without data loss (apt cache, font cache, etc.).

name: Persistent state
description: Persistent application state data: dpkg database, MySQL data files, Docker layers.

name: Lock files
description: Lock files preventing concurrent access to devices or files. Typically symlinked to /run/lock.

name: Logs
description: System and application log files: syslog, auth.log, kern.log, journal (journald), nginx/apache access logs.

name: Spool
description: Spooled data awaiting processing: print spools, cron jobs, mail queues.

name: Persistent temp
description: Temporary files preserved between reboots (unlike /tmp). Used for data that needs to survive a reboot but is still temporary.
